{% raw %}

Sources & notes

Nudging, boosting & transparency

  1. Johnson, E. J. & Goldstein, D. (2003). Do Defaults Save Lives?. Science 302(5649), 1338–1339.The organ-donation default study: countries where donor consent is the form's default (opt-out) show far higher effective consent rates than opt-in countries — the two distributions do not even overlap — so the printed default quietly steers national outcomes.
  2. Thaler, R. H. & Sunstein, C. R. (2008). Nudge: Improving Decisions About Health, Wealth, and Happiness. Yale University Press (book).The founding statement of the no-neutrality point: some choice architecture is unavoidable, so the design of choices always influences them.
  3. Vallier, K. (2016). On the Inevitability of Nudging. Georgetown Journal of Law & Public Policy 14:817.Examines the Thaler–Sunstein claim that choice architecture is unavoidable and concludes that some shaping of choices is, in a robust sense, inevitable — the field's peace treaty with non-neutrality, argued out in full.
  4. Wikipedia contributors (2026). Nudge theory. Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia.A plain-language primer on nudging — what it is, where it came from, and the debates around it.
  5. Wikipedia contributors (2026). Choice architecture. Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia.A plain-language primer on choice architecture — how the presentation of options shapes what gets chosen.

Manipulation & the philosophy of influence

  1. Schmidt, A. T. & Engelen, B. (2020). The Ethics of Nudging: An Overview. Philosophy Compass 15(4).Survey of the decade-long debate; organizes the objections (autonomy, manipulation, transparency) and the replies, including the argument that there is no neutral, influence-free choice architecture to retreat to. (Paywalled to automated verification; the quoted phrase was supplied from the publisher's version.)
  2. Susser, D., Roessler, B. & Nissenbaum, H. (2019). Online Manipulation: Hidden Influences in a Digital World. Georgetown Law Technology Review 4(1).Defines manipulation as covertly influencing decision-making by targeting decision-making vulnerabilities; hiddenness is the core wrong, because it subverts the capacity to form decisions one can endorse as one's own.
  3. Susser, D., Roessler, B. & Nissenbaum, H. (2019). Technology, Autonomy, and Manipulation. Internet Policy Review 8(2).Grounds the harm of manipulation in relational, socially-situated accounts of autonomy rather than an influence-free ideal of the self.
{% endraw %}
